ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori prawn; FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific tandoori-prepared prawn code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 refers to prawn as a substance; no preparation-specific tandoori prawn concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately one large king prawn (about 40 g edible weight after grilling), as typically served per piece at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Tandoori Prawn / Grilled Prawn (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for grilled/tandoori prawn preparations. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for tandoori-style marinated and grilled prawns. Where specific tandoori prawn values were not individually listed, the closest ICMR-NIN entry for cooked prawn with spice marinade was used and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) to confirm macro plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori prawn. Prawns and shrimp are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. Per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care), seafood and shellfish with negligible carbohydrate content have an estimated GI of approximately 5 (effectively zero glycemic impact). GI set to 5 as the lowest defensible positive estimate for a protein-dominant, near-zero-carbohydrate food. GL per serving = (5 × 3.2 × 0.40) / 100 = 0.06, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Tandoori prawn contains approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g due to the spiced marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two pieces and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a negligible glycemic index, tandoori prawn has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable high-protein option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate cholesterol; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori prawn provides lean protein with only 1.1 g saturated fat per 100 g; while cholesterol content is moderate (~155 mg/100 g), the low saturated fat profile and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids make it a heart-friendly choice when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Prawn is a classic North Indian dish consisting of large king prawns marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically containing tandoori masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven or over charcoal. The result is a prawn with a lightly charred, smoky exterior, tender and juicy flesh, and a complex spiced flavour profile that is simultaneously smoky, tangy, and mildly spicy. In Singapore, tandoori prawn is commonly served at Indian restaurants and is often sold per piece, reflecting the premium size of the king prawns used. Nutritionally, tandoori prawn is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 22.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it highly suitable for low-glycaemic and high-protein dietary patterns. The fat content is moderate at 4.8 g per 100 g, with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio. Sodium content is elevated due to the marinade (approximately 520 mg/100 g), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal, and is rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and zinc — nutrients important for immune function, neurological health, and bone metabolism. Clinically, it is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact, and for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawn?
Is tandoori prawn good for diabetics?
Is tandoori prawn high in protein?
What spices are in tandoori prawn marinade?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for tandoori/grilled prawn preparations and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data. No single exact ICMR-NIN entry for 'tandoori king prawn per piece' was available; values represent the best defensible estimate from the closest approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on the near-zero carbohydrate content of prawns per University of Sydney GI Database methodology.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as prawns and their typical marinades do not contain industrially produced trans fats — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN. Serving size of 40 g is an estimate for one large king prawn (edible portion after grilling); actual restaurant portion may vary. Sodium value reflects the marinated and grilled preparation and may vary by restaurant recipe. The food is sold per piece at Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source). GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a near-zero-carbohydrate protein food; glycemic load per serving is 0.1.

improvement_actions_taken: Added all five mandatory Indian cuisine local_names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Populated nutrient values from ICMR-NIN adapted source cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central.
Set GI to 5 (analogous estimate, low category) with University of Sydney GI Database methodology citation.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Verified gi_category = 'low' is correct for GI = 5 (≤55 threshold).
Used only match_type values: 'exact', 'closest_parent', 'none' — all ontology match types are 'closest_parent' which is valid.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions with Type 2 Diabetes human_condition_name.
